EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow but heavy band of snow focused along the Interstate 94 corridor between the cities of Madison and Milwaukee on the evening of January 16. Periods of 0.5 to 1.0+ inch per hour snowfall rates within the snow band resulted in generally 2 to 5 inches of accumulation in locations impacted by the snow band. A few locally higher totals approaching 6 inches were reported in western portions of Milwaukee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulation focusing over the southern half of the county. Locally higher totals between 3 and 4 inches over extreme southern parts of the county. Highest accumulation of 4.0 inches observed in Watertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of light to moderate snow developed over parts of southern Wisconsin from the late evening hours of January 20 into the early morning hours of January 21. Snow brought generally 2 to 3 inches of accumulation to locations along and south of the Interstate 94 and US-18 corridors. Locally higher totals to 4 inches occurred in far southwestern Wisconsin. While snow had concluded, slippery roads continued into the morning commute on January 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1.5 to 2 inches of snow accumulation observed countywide, with highest totals over the southern half of the county. Highest accumulation of 2 inches observed in Mount Horeb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow but heavy band of snow developed near the Wisconsin-Illinois border during the evening hours. Enhanced hourly snowfall rates within the snow band led to snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches, with locally higher totals. Overlapping with the evening commute, the snowfall led to slippery road conditions. Winds became gusty after the snow ended, leading to blowing snow and reduced visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2 to 4 inches of snow observed over far southern parts of the county. Highest total of 4 inches observed near Brooklyn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Arctic cold front quickly moved across southern Wisconsin during the early morning hours, bringing a burst of moderate to heavy snow to the many locations along and east of Interstate 39. While snow accumulation was minimal, the brief moderate to heavy snowfall rates combined with gusty winds to significantly reduce visibilities. With high temperatures in the 50s the day prior, flash freezing of roadways also occurred. Significant morning commute impacts occurred as a result of the conditions, with numerous crashes reported on across the impacted area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation generally an inch or less countywide; numerous reported crashes on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system brought a period of snow to northeast Wisconsin from mid morning through the early evening on Saturday (1/10) along with gusty northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph. Although much of the area saw a period without snowfall, northern Door County stayed in the moderate snow for much of the day.  Snowfall amounts of 6 to 8 inches were noted across northern Door County by Saturday (1/10) afternoon.  The highest snowfall amounts were 8 inches in Ellison Bay and 7.5 inches in Sister Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall totals across Door County included 8 inches in Ellison Bay and 7.5 inches in Sister Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and strong upper wave of low pressure led to a quick swath of snow over southeast WI. 1-3 inches of snow accumulation led to slick roads and many vehicle slide-offs and accidents. Much of the snow accumulation occurred in a 2 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 1-3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following an Arctic cold front passage on the afternoon of January 22, a bitterly cold air mass settled into southern Wisconsin and much of the Upper Midwest. Air temperatures in the single digits to teens below zero would reach southern Wisconsin by the morning of January 23, combining with brisk northwest winds to create widespread wind chills between 35 and 45 below. The conditions prompted Extreme Cold Warnings across all of southern Wisconsin, with most school districts canceling classes on January 23. The school cancellations extended all the way up to the university level, with both the University of Wisconsin-Madison and Marquette University among major colleges electing to close on January 23. Extreme cold conditions continued over portions of central and east-central Wisconsin into the morning of January 24.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind chills between 35 and 40 below zero countywide. Coldest wind chill of 41 below observed at the Madison ASOS.

Coldest wind chill of 43 below observed at Lost Lake on the morning of 01/23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) continued into the New Year across portions of north-central and northeast Wisconsin. A strong storm brought copious amounts of rain and some snow on the 8th and 9th. Unusually high rainfall amounts for January between 1 and 2.25 inches were recorded in a band from Wisconsin Rapids northeast through Antigo and Shawano to Marinette and Wausaukee. The heavy rain and snow brought an end to the Severe Drought (D2) conditions across many counties by the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation totals for the fall (Sep-Nov) were only 40-60% of normal. In January, a significant storm brought one to two inches of rain and a little snow to the county on the 8th into the 9th. The heavy precipitation brought an end to the Severe Drought (D2) on the January 20th Drought Monitor. For the month, precipitation totals were at least 150-200% of normal. By the end of the month, the entire county was designated in Moderate Drought (D1) due to long term precipitation deficits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass brought very cold temperatures to the entire region Thursday evening (1/22) through Saturday morning (1/24).  Winds of 15 to 25 mph combined with low temperatures of 15 to 30 below zero brought wind chill values down to 30 to 55 below zero Thursday evening (1/22) through Friday morning (1/23). Temperatures did not warm much Friday afternoon, with daytime highs ranging from 5 to 15 below zero, offering little relief from the cold, with wind chill values at or near extreme cold criteria, even in the afternoon. Plummeting overnight temperatures Friday night (1/23) into Saturday morning (1/24) of 15 to near 40 below zero once again pushed wind chill values down to 30 to near 50 below zero. Temperatures had finally warmed to above extreme cold criteria late Saturday morning (1/24) but remained cold through the weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold temperatures and gusty winds caused wind chill values to fall to -35 to -54 degrees from Thursday evening (1/22) through early Saturday morning (1/24).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold temperatures and gusty winds caused wind chill values to fall to -30 to -41 degrees from late Thursday evening (1/22) through Saturday morning (1/24).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure brought another surge of cold air to the region late Sunday night (1/25) into Monday morning (1/26). A few locations across north-central Wisconsin once again dropped to extreme cold criteria Monday morning (1/26) as cold temperatures of 20 to 30 below zero and light westerly winds of 5 to 10 mph pushed wind chill values down to 35 to nearly 40 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold temperatures and light winds caused wind chill values to fall to -35 to -39 degrees Monday morning (1/26).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of northwestern Wisconsin during the month of January. No changes in drought status were observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for the month was above normal across the northeastern half of Douglas County with below normal conditions elsewhere. The dry period in place extended back to last summer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions covered much of the western 3/4ths of the county during the month of January. No changes in drought category were observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for the month was up to 0.50 inches below normal across the southwestern half of the county, including Superior, with above normal precipitation up to 0.50 inches above normal across the northeastern half of the county. The dry pattern in place extended back into the summer months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of dangerously cold air spread across the Upper Midwest behind a series of strong arctic fronts from January 21 through January 23. Wind chills dropped into the -40s across western Wisconsin, with RCX in Ladysmith reporting the coldest wind chill of -49F during this stretch of extreme cold. The strongest winds did not line up with the coolest temperatures, limiting wind chills from getting any colder than -49F.||The peak of the coldest conditions occurred from the evening on the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. Very cold air temperatures around -10 to -20 below continued until the morning of the 26th, though winds were generally at or below 10 mph, limiting wind chills from dropping below -35F.||The lowest wind chills were:|-49F at KRCX (Rusk).|-47F at KRNH (St Croix).|-45F at KEAU (Chippewa).|-43F at KUBE (Barron).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KRNH reported wind gusts as high as 30 mph during the evening as air temperatures dropped from -15F to -25F overnight. The lowest measured wind chill was -47F at 4:30AM on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second wave of wintry precipitation occurred 24 hours after a light mix of snow and freezing rain fell the day prior. This round was heavier and temperatures were right around freezing, leading to a mixture of snow, freezing rain, and rain. For most locations, the main period of freezing rain occurred very early in the morning on January 6th as rainfall rates were lighter. Rates then increased throughout the morning of the 6th which made it difficult for ice accretion to continue as temperatures hovered right around the freezing point. During the morning commute, most areas saw more of a crunchy slush on the ground as the heavier rain attempted to melt the coating of ice that developed overnight. The heaviest impacted area was St Croix, Polk, and Barron counties where temperatures were slightly cooler and the rain rates were not as heavy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at KRPD and KUBE both reported several hours of freezing precipitation during the early morning hours of the 6th. A CoCoRaHS observer also reported freezing rain in Rice Lake.

Rates then increased throughout the morning of the 6th which made it difficult for ice accretion to continue as temperatures hovered right around the freezing point. During the morning commute, most areas saw more of a crunchy slush on the ground as the heavier rain attempted to melt the coating of ice that developed overnight. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of dangerously cold air spread across the Upper Midwest behind a series of strong arctic fronts from January 21 through January 23. The strongest winds did not line up with the coolest temperatures, limiting wind chills from getting any colder than -49F.||The peak of the coldest conditions occurred from the evening on the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. Very cold air temperatures around -10 to -20 below continued until the morning of the 26th, though winds were generally at or below 10 mph, limiting wind chills from dropping below -35F.||The lowest wind chills were:|-49F at KRCX (Rusk).|-47F at KRNH (St Croix).|-45F at KEAU (Chippewa).|-43F at KUBE (Barron).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KRCX reported wind gusts as high as 30 mph during the evening as air temperatures dropped from -15F to -27F overnight. The lowest measured wind chill was -49F at 5AM on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass brought very cold temperatures to the entire region Thursday evening (1/22) through Saturday morning (1/24).  Winds of 15 to 25 mph combined with low temperatures of 15 to 30 below zero brought wind chill values down to 30 to 55 below zero Thursday evening (1/22) through Friday morning (1/23). Temperatures did not warm much Friday afternoon, with daytime highs ranging from 5 to 15 below zero, offering little relief from the cold, with wind chill values at or near extreme cold criteria, even in the afternoon. Plummeting overnight temperatures Friday night (1/23) into Saturday morning (1/24) of 15 to near 40 below zero once again pushed wind chill values down to 30 to near 50 below zero. Temperatures had finally warmed to above extreme cold criteria late Saturday morning (1/24) but remained cold through the weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold temperatures and gusty winds caused wind chill values to fall to -35 to -53 degrees from Thursday evening (1/22) through Saturday morning (1/24).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake-effect snowfall in the wake of a clipper system on the 16th into the 17th led to heavy snowfall along the South Shore of Lake Superior. Accumulations of 6 to 9 inches were reported near Lake Superior with a high report of 13.5 inches in northern Douglas County where a gravity wave band set up during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall lasted just over 24 hours with this system and allowed snow to pile up. A gravity wave set up across the northern part of the county as well which added to the snowfall totals. Accumulations of 6 to 9 inches were reported with a high report of 13.5 inches near Hawthorne.

Many locations saw low temperatures colder than 20 degrees below zero with widespread wind chills ranging between 30 to 55 degrees below zero across western Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of extreme cold moved into the region beginning on January 22nd and continued into January 23rd. During this period extremely cold wind chills of 35 to 45 degrees below occurred in Adams County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather system resulted in a light wintry mix of precipitation during the afternoon of January 4th. Surface temperatures were about 5 to 10 degrees below freezing, under a slightly warmer air mass just above the surface. This resulted in a few hours of light freezing rain or drizzle, and ultimately light ice accretion, creating slick surfaces for a number of counties. The farther north, the airmass was cooler just above the surface, resulting in more snow mixing in with any freezing precipitation. This limited impacts for Polk, Barron, and Rusk counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observers reported freezing rain and a glaze of ice near Ellsworth and Spring Valley.
